title.zh: 卷十四 宗室四王三侯列傳
title.en: Volume 14: Biographies of the Imperial Clan, Four Princes and Three Marquises
title.raw: 卷十四 宗室四王三侯列傳 Volume 14: Biographies of the Imperial Clan, Four Princes and Three Marquises

sentences.zh: 齊武王

translations.literal: Prince Martial of Qi
translations.idiomatic: Prince Martial of Qi (Liu Yan)

sentences.zh: 齊武王縯字伯升，光武之長兄也。

translations.literal: Prince Martial of Qi, Yan, styled Bosheng, was Emperor Guangwu’s eldest elder brother.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Yan, posthumously honored as Prince Martial of Qi and known by his courtesy name Bosheng, was Guangwu’s eldest brother.

sentences.zh: 性剛毅，慷慨有大節。

translations.literal: By nature he was firm and resolute, high-minded and possessed of great integrity.
translations.idiomatic: He was by nature stern and resolute, magnanimous in spirit, and capable of great constancy.

sentences.zh: 自王莽篡漢，常憤憤，懷復社稷之慮，不事家人居業，傾身破產，交結天下雄俊。

translations.literal: From the time Wang Mang usurped the Han, he was constantly indignant, harbored thoughts of restoring the altars of soil and grain, did not attend to household affairs or settled property, exhausted his person and ruined his estate, and associated with the outstanding heroes of the realm.
translations.idiomatic: After Wang Mang seized the Han throne, he nursed a smoldering anger and dreamed of restoring the dynasty; he neglected family business and ran through his fortune to win the friendship of bold men from every quarter.

sentences.zh: 莽末，盜賊群起，南方尤甚。

translations.literal: At the end of the Mang era, bandits rose in swarms, especially severe in the south.
translations.idiomatic: In the closing years of Wang Mang’s rule, rebels sprang up everywhere, and the south was worst afflicted.

sentences.zh: 伯升召諸豪傑計議曰：『王莽暴虐，百姓分崩。

translations.literal: Bosheng summoned the various stalwarts to plan and consult, saying, “Wang Mang is cruel and oppressive; the common people are scattered and broken.
translations.idiomatic: Bosheng called the local notables to council and told them, “Wang Mang is a tyrant, and the people have been driven from their homes.

sentences.zh: 今枯旱連年，兵革並起。

translations.literal: Now drought has continued year after year, and arms and armor have risen together everywhere.
translations.idiomatic: Year after year the land is parched, and everywhere weapons are being taken up.

sentences.zh: 此亦天亡之時，復高祖之業，定萬世之秋也。

translations.literal: This too is the moment when Heaven is destroying him; it is the season to restore the enterprise of the High Ancestor and fix the destiny for ten thousand generations.”
translations.idiomatic: This is Heaven’s appointed hour to cast him down—to revive Gaozu’s work and secure an inheritance that will last for ages.”

sentences.zh: 』眾皆然之。

translations.literal: ” The crowd all approved it.
translations.idiomatic: The assembly agreed.

sentences.zh: 於是分遣親客，使鄧晨起新野，光武與李通、李軼起於宛。

translations.literal: Thereupon he separately dispatched trusted clients, sent Deng Chen to raise troops at Xinye, and Guangwu with Li Tong and Li Yi to rise at Wan.
translations.idiomatic: He then sent his confidants on separate missions: Deng Chen was to raise Xinye, while Guangwu joined Li Tong and Li Yi in revolt at Wan.

sentences.zh: 伯升自發舂陵子弟，合七八千人，部署賓客，自稱柱天都部。

translations.literal: Bosheng himself mobilized the youths of Chunling, totaling seven or eight thousand men, arranged his retainers, and styled himself commander of the “Pillar of Heaven” host.
translations.idiomatic: From Chunling he personally mustered seven or eight thousand clansmen, organized his followers, and took the title commander of the Pillar-of-Heaven column.

sentences.zh: 使宗室劉嘉往誘新市、平林兵王匡、陳牧等，合軍而進，屠長聚及唐子鄉，殺湖陽尉，進拔棘陽，因欲攻宛。

translations.literal: He sent a member of the imperial clan, Liu Jia, to go entice the Xinshi and Pinglin forces under Wang Kuang, Chen Mu, and others; they combined their armies and advanced, slaughtered Changju and Tangzi township, killed the commandant of Huyang, advanced and took Jiyang, and thereupon intended to attack Wan.
translations.idiomatic: He dispatched Liu Jia of the imperial house to win over Wang Kuang, Chen Mu, and the rest of the Xinshi and Pinglin armies; united, they marched, put Changju and Tangzi to the sword, slew the Huyang district officer, seized Jiyang, and prepared to strike Wan.

sentences.zh: 至小長安，與王莽前隊大夫酎阜、屬正梁丘賜戰。

translations.literal: When they reached Little Chang’an, they fought with Zhou Fu, front-rank grandee under Wang Mang, and Liangqiu Ci, adjunct rectifier.
translations.idiomatic: At Little Chang’an they gave battle to Zhou Fu, Wang Mang’s front-rank grandee, and Liangqiu Ci, his adjunct rectifier.

sentences.zh: 時天密霧，漢軍大敗，姊元弟仲皆遇害，宗從死者數十人。

translations.literal: At the time the sky was thick with fog; the Han army suffered a great defeat; his elder sister Yuan and younger brother Zhong were both killed in the fighting, and several tens of kinsmen who followed perished.
translations.idiomatic: A dense fog blanketed the field; the Han forces were routed. His sister Yuan and brother Zhong fell in the melee, and dozens of kinsmen died with them.

sentences.zh: 伯升復收會兵眾，還保棘陽。

translations.literal: Bosheng again gathered and assembled the troops and returned to hold Jiyang.
translations.idiomatic: Bosheng rallied his men and withdrew to defend Jiyang.

sentences.zh: 阜、賜乘勝，留輜重於藍鄉，引精兵十萬南渡黃淳水，臨沘水，阻兩川間為營，絕後橋，示無還心。

translations.literal: Fu and Ci, riding their victory, left baggage trains at Lanxiang, led picked troops of one hundred thousand south across the Huangchun River, came to the Zhi River, blocked the space between the two streams to make a camp, cut the bridge behind them, and showed that they had no thought of return.
translations.idiomatic: Fu and Ci pressed their advantage: they parked their supply wagons at Lanxiang, marched a hundred thousand elite soldiers south across the Huangchun, drew up on the Zhi between the two watercourses, destroyed the rear bridge, and made plain that they would not retreat.

sentences.zh: 新市、平林見漢兵數敗，阜、賜軍大至，各欲解去，伯升甚患之。

translations.literal: The Xinshi and Pinglin forces, seeing that the Han troops had repeatedly been defeated and that Fu and Ci’s army had arrived in great strength, each wished to break away and leave; Bosheng was greatly troubled by it.
translations.idiomatic: The Xinshi and Pinglin contingents, demoralized by repeated defeats and daunted by Fu and Ci’s host, wanted to scatter; Bosheng was deeply alarmed.

sentences.zh: 會下江兵五千餘人至宜秋，乃往為說合從之勢，下江從之。

translations.literal: It happened that more than five thousand men of the Lower Yang army reached Yiqiu; he then went and expounded to them the advantage of a joint vertical alliance, and the Lower Yang followed him.
translations.idiomatic: Just then over five thousand Lower Yang troops arrived at Yiqiu; he went in person to argue for a united front, and they accepted his plan.

sentences.zh: 語在《王常傳》。

translations.literal: The account is given in the biography of Wang Chang.
translations.idiomatic: The full story is told in the biography of Wang Chang.

sentences.zh: 伯升於是大饗軍士，設盟約。

translations.literal: Bosheng thereupon greatly feasted the soldiers and established a covenant by oath.
translations.idiomatic: Bosheng then feasted the army at length and bound them with a blood oath.

sentences.zh: 休卒三日，分為六部，潛師夜起，襲取藍鄉，盡獲其輜重。

translations.literal: They rested the troops three days, divided into six divisions, secretly moved the army by night, raided and took Lanxiang, and captured all their baggage trains in full.
translations.idiomatic: After three days’ rest the force split into six columns, stole out by night, stormed Lanxiang, and carried off the enemy’s entire baggage.

sentences.zh: 明旦，漢軍自西南攻酎阜，下江兵自東南攻梁攻丘賜。

translations.literal: At dawn the Han army from the southwest attacked Zhou Fu, and the Lower Yang troops from the southeast attacked Liangqiu Ci.
translations.idiomatic: At daybreak the Han troops struck Zhou Fu from the southwest while the Lower Yang men hit Liangqiu Ci from the southeast.

sentences.zh: 至食時，賜陳潰，阜軍望見散走，漢兵急追之，卻迫黃淳水，斬首溺死者二萬餘人，遂斬阜、賜。

translations.literal: By the time of the meal, Ci’s formation broke; Fu’s army, seeing it, scattered and fled; the Han troops urgently pursued them, pressed them back against the Huangchun River, and those beheaded or drowned numbered more than twenty thousand; thereupon they beheaded Fu and Ci.
translations.idiomatic: By mid-morning Ci’s line had collapsed; Fu’s men broke and ran. The Han army pressed the pursuit, drove them against the Huangchun, and killed more than twenty thousand by blade or drowning, then cut down Fu and Ci themselves.

sentences.zh: 王莽納言將軍嚴尤、秩宗將軍陳茂聞阜、賜軍敗，引欲據宛。

translations.literal: Yan You, spokesman general under Wang Mang, and Chen Mao, ritual patriarch general, heard that Fu and Ci’s army had been defeated and led their forces intending to occupy Wan.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Mang’s spokesman general Yan You and ritual patriarch Chen Mao, learning of Fu and Ci’s defeat, marched to seize Wan.

sentences.zh: 伯升乃陳兵誓眾，焚積聚，破釜甑，鼓行而前，與尤、茂遇育陽下，戰，大破之，斬首三千餘級。

translations.literal: Bosheng then arrayed his troops, swore the host, burned the accumulated stores, smashed the cauldrons and steamers, beat the drums and advanced, met You and Mao below Yuyang, fought, greatly defeated them, and beheaded more than three thousand grades.
translations.idiomatic: Bosheng drew up his ranks, harangued the men, burned the grain depots, broke the cooking vessels, and advanced with drums rolling; below Yuyang he met You and Mao, shattered them, and took more than three thousand heads.

sentences.zh: 尤、茂棄軍走，伯升遂進圍宛，自號柱天大將軍。

translations.literal: You and Mao abandoned the army and fled; Bosheng thereupon advanced and besieged Wan, styling himself great general of the Pillar of Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: You and Mao fled the field, and Bosheng pressed on to invest Wan, proclaiming himself great general of the Pillar of Heaven.

sentences.zh: 王莽素聞其名，大震懼，購伯升邑五萬戶，黃金十萬斤，位上公。

translations.literal: Wang Mang had long heard his name and was greatly shaken with fear; he offered a reward on Bosheng’s head of a domain of fifty thousand households, one hundred thousand jin of gold, and rank equal to the upper dukes.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Mang, who had long known his reputation, was terrified and posted a price on his head—fifty thousand households, a hundred thousand jin of gold, and status on a par with the highest nobles.

sentences.zh: 使長安中官署及天下鄉亭皆畫伯升像於塾，旦起射之。

translations.literal: He ordered that in the central offices at Chang’an and in every township post throughout the realm they should paint Bosheng’s likeness on the gate screen and shoot at it each morning at rising.
translations.idiomatic: He commanded every yamen in Chang’an and every rural post in the empire to paint Bosheng’s portrait on the schoolhouse wall and shoot at it at dawn.

sentences.zh: 自阜、賜死後，百姓日有降者，眾至十餘萬。

translations.literal: From the time Fu and Ci died, commoners daily came over to surrender, and the multitude reached more than a hundred thousand.
translations.idiomatic: After Fu and Ci fell, townsfolk flocked in daily to submit, until the host numbered well over a hundred thousand.

sentences.zh: 諸將會議立劉氏以從人望，豪傑咸歸於伯升，而新市、平林將帥樂放縱，憚怕升威明而貪聖公懦弱，先共定策立之，然後使騎召伯升，示其議。

translations.literal: The generals met in council to establish a member of the Liu clan in order to comply with popular expectation; the stalwarts all turned to Bosheng, but the Xinshi and Pinglin commanders delighted in license and debauchery, feared Bosheng’s stern authority yet coveted Shengong’s weakness, first together fixed a plan to establish him, then afterward sent horsemen to summon Bosheng and show him their decision.
translations.idiomatic: The chiefs resolved to put a Liu on the throne to satisfy public sentiment; the heroes looked to Bosheng, but the Xinshi and Pinglin leaders loved their unruly ways—they shrank from Bosheng’s iron discipline and preferred the pliable Shengong—so they settled the succession among themselves, then rode to fetch Bosheng and inform him of what they had done.

sentences.zh: 伯升曰：『諸將軍幸欲尊立宗室，其德甚厚，然愚鄙之見，竊有未同。

translations.literal: Bosheng said, “You generals, in wishing to honor and establish a member of the imperial clan, your virtue is very great; yet in my foolish and base view, I privately have a point on which I do not agree.
translations.idiomatic: Bosheng replied, “Your wish to raise a scion of the house shows great public spirit; still, with all respect, I cannot quite concur.

sentences.zh: 今赤眉起青、徐，眾數十萬，聞南陽立宗室，恐赤眉復有所立，如此，必將內爭。

translations.literal: Now the Red Eyebrows have risen in Qing and Xu, their numbers several hundred thousand; if they hear that Nanyang has established a member of the imperial clan, I fear the Red Eyebrows will again establish someone; in such a case there will certainly be internal strife.
translations.idiomatic: The Red Eyebrows have already risen in Qing and Xu with hundreds of thousands of followers. If they learn that Nanyang has enthroned a Liu, they may set up another claimant, and we shall tear one another apart.

sentences.zh: 今王莽未滅，而宗室相攻，是疑天下而自損權，非所以破莽也。

translations.literal: Now Wang Mang is not yet destroyed, yet members of the imperial clan attack one another; this is to make the realm doubtful and diminish one’s own authority; it is not the way to break Wang Mang.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Mang still lives; for imperial kinsmen to turn on each other would unsettle the empire and sap our own strength—it is no way to finish Wang Mang.

sentences.zh: 且首兵唱號，鮮有能遂，陳勝、項籍，即其事也。

translations.literal: Moreover, those who first raise arms and proclaim a title have rarely been able to carry it through—Chen Sheng and Xiang Ji are examples of the matter.”
translations.idiomatic: Besides, the first man to seize the drumstick and cry kingship seldom lasts—Chen Sheng and Xiang Yu are proof enough.”

sentences.zh: 舂陵去宛三百里耳，未足為功。

translations.literal: Chunling is only three hundred li from Wan; it is not sufficient to count as merit.
translations.idiomatic: Chunling lies a mere three hundred li from Wan; that is hardly a feat to boast of.

sentences.zh: 遽自尊立，為天下準的，使後人得承吾敝，非計之善者也。

translations.literal: Hastily to honor oneself as emperor is to become the world’s target and let those who come after exploit our exhaustion; it is not a good plan.
translations.idiomatic: To leap at once to the imperial title is to paint a target on our backs and invite later rivals to profit from our weariness—poor strategy.

sentences.zh: 今且稱王以號令。

translations.literal: For now let us style ourselves kings in order to issue commands.
translations.idiomatic: For the present let us take the title of king and issue orders in that name.

sentences.zh: 若赤眉所立者賢，相率而往從之；

translations.literal: If the man the Red Eyebrows set up proves worthy, let us lead our forces together and go follow him;
translations.idiomatic: If their choice proves worthy, we can join him in due course;

sentences.zh: 若無所立，破莽降赤眉，然後舉尊號，亦未晚也。

translations.literal: if they establish no one, then after we destroy Wang Mang and bring the Red Eyebrows to submission, we may then raise the supreme title, and it will still not be late.
translations.idiomatic: if they raise no one, we crush Wang Mang and subdue the Red Eyebrows, then take the supreme title—there will still be time.

sentences.zh: 願各詳思之。

translations.literal: I beg each of you to consider it carefully.
translations.idiomatic: Think it through, every one of you.”

sentences.zh: 』諸將多曰『善』。

translations.literal: ” The generals for the most part said “Good.”
translations.idiomatic: Most of the generals cried, “Well said!”

sentences.zh: 將軍張卬拔劍擊地曰：『疑事無功。

translations.literal: General Zhang Ang drew his sword, struck the ground, and said, “In doubtful affairs there is no achievement.
translations.idiomatic: General Zhang Ang smashed his sword on the earth and shouted, “Hesitation wins nothing.

sentences.zh: 今日之議，不得有二。

translations.literal: In today’s deliberation there may not be two.”
translations.idiomatic: Today’s debate allows no second course.”

sentences.zh: 』眾皆從之。

translations.literal: ” The crowd all followed him.
translations.idiomatic: The assembly bowed to his verdict.

sentences.zh: 聖公既即位，拜伯升為大司徒，封漢信侯。

translations.literal: After Shengong had already ascended the throne, he appointed Bosheng grand minister over the masses and enfeoffed him as marquis of Hanxin.
translations.idiomatic: Once Shengong was enthroned, he named Bosheng grand minister over the masses and marquis of Hanxin.

sentences.zh: 由是豪傑失望，多不服。

translations.literal: Thereupon the stalwarts were disappointed, and many were not compliant.
translations.idiomatic: The bold spirits were bitterly disappointed, and many refused to obey.

sentences.zh: 平林後部攻新野，不能下。

translations.literal: The rear division of Pinglin attacked Xinye but could not take it.
translations.idiomatic: Pinglin’s rear corps besieged Xinye without success.

sentences.zh: 新野宰登城言曰：『得司徒劉公一信，願先下。

translations.literal: The magistrate of Xinye mounted the city wall and said, “If I obtain one pledge from Minister over the Masses Liu, I am willing to submit first.”
translations.idiomatic: The magistrate of Xinye stood on the wall and called out, “Give me a single word from Minister Liu, and I will open the gates at once.”

sentences.zh: 』及伯升軍至，即開城門降。

translations.literal: ” When Bosheng’s army arrived, he immediately opened the city gates and surrendered.
translations.idiomatic: When Bosheng’s host appeared, he threw open the city and yielded.

sentences.zh: 五月，伯升拔宛。

translations.literal: In the fifth month Bosheng took Wan.
translations.idiomatic: In the fifth month Bosheng captured Wan.

sentences.zh: 六月，光武破王尋、王邑。

translations.literal: In the sixth month Guangwu defeated Wang Xun and Wang Yi.
translations.idiomatic: In the sixth month Guangwu destroyed Wang Xun and Wang Yi at Kunyang.

sentences.zh: 自是兄弟威名益甚。

translations.literal: From this time the brothers’ martial renown grew ever greater.
translations.idiomatic: Thereafter the two brothers’ fame as commanders spread far and wide.

sentences.zh: 更始君臣不自安，遂共謀誅伯升，乃大會諸將，以成其計。

translations.literal: The lord and ministers of Gengshi were not at ease in themselves, thereupon together plotted to execute Bosheng, and then greatly assembled the generals in order to carry out the plan.
translations.idiomatic: Gengshi and his court, uneasy at Bosheng’s power, conspired to kill him and summoned a great council of generals as cover.

sentences.zh: 更始取伯升寶劍視之，繡衣御史申屠建隨獻玉玦，更始竟不能發。

translations.literal: Gengshi took Bosheng’s precious sword to examine it; the embroidered-gown censor Shentu Jian thereupon presented a jade ring, yet Gengshi in the end could not bring himself to act.
translations.idiomatic: Gengshi borrowed Bosheng’s sword to inspect it; Shentu Jian, censor in the embroidered robe, offered the signal jade ring, but the emperor still could not strike.

sentences.zh: 及罷會，伯升舅樊宏謂伯升曰：『昔鴻門之會，范增舉玦以示項羽。

translations.literal: When the meeting dispersed, Bosheng’s maternal uncle Fan Hong said to Bosheng, “At the feast at Hongmen, Fan Zeng raised the jade ring to show Xiang Yu.
translations.idiomatic: After the council Fan Hong, Bosheng’s uncle, warned him, “At Hongmen Fan Zeng lifted his jade ring to prompt Xiang Yu.

sentences.zh: 今建此意，得無不善乎？

translations.literal: Now Jian’s intention in this—can it be other than ill?”
translations.idiomatic: Jian’s gesture today—does it not mean the same?”

sentences.zh: 』伯升笑而不應。

translations.literal: ” Bosheng smiled and did not answer.
translations.idiomatic: Bosheng only smiled and said nothing.

sentences.zh: 初，李軼諂事更始貴將，光武深疑之，常以戒伯升曰：『此人不可復信。

translations.literal: In the beginning Li Yi fawned on Gengshi’s favored generals; Guangwu deeply doubted him and often used it to warn Bosheng, saying, “This person cannot be trusted again.”
translations.idiomatic: Earlier, Li Yi had curried favor with Gengshi’s inner circle; Guangwu distrusted him deeply and had often urged Bosheng, “Never trust that man again.”

sentences.zh: 』又不受。

translations.literal: Again he did not accept.
translations.idiomatic: Bosheng still would not heed him.

sentences.zh: 伯升部將宗人劉稷，數陷陳潰圍，勇冠三軍。

translations.literal: Bosheng’s subordinate general, a clansman Liu Ji, repeatedly broke enemy formations and smashed sieges; his courage crowned the three armies.
translations.idiomatic: Among Bosheng’s officers his kinsman Liu Ji again and again shattered enemy lines and relieved encirclements; his valor outshone the whole army.

sentences.zh: 時將兵擊魯陽，聞更始立，怒曰：『本起兵圖大事者，伯升兄弟也，今更始何為者邪？

translations.literal: At the time he was leading troops to strike Luyang; hearing that Gengshi had been established, he angrily said, “Those who originally raised arms to plan the great affair were the Bosheng brothers; now what business has Gengshi?”
translations.idiomatic: He was campaigning against Luyang when news came that Gengshi had been enthroned; he fumed, “We took up arms for a great cause—the Bosheng brothers led us—so what is this Gengshi?”

sentences.zh: 』更始君臣聞而心忌之，以稷為抗威將軍，稷不肯拜。

translations.literal: ” The lord and ministers of Gengshi heard it and harbored jealousy in their hearts; they appointed Ji resisting-power general, but Ji refused to accept the appointment.
translations.idiomatic: The courtiers of Gengshi, hearing this, hated him; they named him resisting-power general, yet he would not take the seal.

sentences.zh: 更始乃與諸將陳兵數千人，先收稷，將誅之，伯升固爭。

translations.literal: Gengshi thereupon with the various generals arrayed troops numbering several thousand men, first seized Ji, intending to execute him; Bosheng firmly remonstrated.
translations.idiomatic: Gengshi then marched out several thousand guards, seized Ji first, and prepared to behead him; Bosheng argued fiercely on his behalf.

sentences.zh: 李軼、朱鮪因勸更始並執伯升，即日害之。

translations.literal: Li Yi and Zhu Wei thereupon urged Gengshi to seize Bosheng together with him, and on the same day harmed them.
translations.idiomatic: Li Yi and Zhu Wei pressed Gengshi to arrest Bosheng as well, and on that very day both men were murdered.

sentences.zh: 有二子。

translations.literal: He had two sons.
translations.idiomatic: He left two sons.

sentences.zh: 建武二年，立長子章為太原王，興為魯王。

translations.literal: In the second year of Jianwu he established his eldest son Zhang as king of Taiyuan and Xing as king of Lu.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 2 the eldest son Liu Zhang was made king of Taiyuan and Liu Xing king of Lu.

sentences.zh: 十一年，徙章為齊王。

translations.literal: In the eleventh year he moved Zhang to be king of Qi.
translations.idiomatic: In the eleventh year Zhang was transferred to the kingdom of Qi.

sentences.zh: 十五年，追謚伯升為齊武王。

translations.literal: In the fifteenth year he posthumously honored Bosheng with the title Prince Martial of Qi.
translations.idiomatic: In the fifteenth year Bosheng was posthumously titled Prince Martial of Qi.

sentences.zh: 章少孤，光武感伯升功業不就，撫育恩愛甚篤，以其少貴，欲令親吏事，故使試守平陰令，遷梁郡太守。

translations.literal: Zhang was orphaned while young; Guangwu, moved that Bosheng’s great work had not been completed, cherished and reared him with very deep kindness; because he was young and noble, wishing to make him personally attend to administrative affairs, therefore he had him probationarily hold the magistracy of Pingyin, then promoted him to governor of Liang commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang lost his father in childhood; Guangwu, grieving that Bosheng’s cause had been cut short, lavished care on him and, because the boy was highborn and still young, wished to school him in government—first as acting magistrate of Pingyin, then as governor of Liang.

sentences.zh: 立二十一年薨，謚曰哀王。

translations.literal: Having reigned twenty-one years he died; his posthumous title was King Ai.
translations.idiomatic: He ruled twenty-one years and died, receiving the posthumous name King Ai (“the Lamented”).

sentences.zh: 子煬王石嗣。

translations.literal: His son, King Yang, Shi, succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Shi, later known as King Yang, succeeded him.

sentences.zh: 建武二十七年，石始就國。

translations.literal: In the twenty-seventh year of Jianwu Shi first went to his kingdom.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 27 Liu Shi went to his fief in person.

sentences.zh: 三十年，封石弟張為下博侯。

translations.literal: In the thirtieth year he enfeoffed Shi’s younger brother Zhang as marquis of Xiabo.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 30 his brother Liu Zhang was made marquis of Xiabo.

sentences.zh: 永平十四年，封石二子為鄉侯。

translations.literal: In the fourteenth year of Yongping he enfeoffed two sons of Shi as village marquises.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongping 14 two of Liu Shi’s sons were enfeoffed as village marquises.

sentences.zh: 石立二十四年薨，子晃嗣。

translations.literal: Shi, having reigned twenty-four years, died; his son Huang succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Shi died after twenty-four years on the throne; his son Huang inherited.

sentences.zh: 下博侯張以善論議，十六年，與奉車都尉竇固等並出擊匈奴，後進者多害其能，數被譖訴。

translations.literal: The marquis of Xiabo, Zhang, because he was skilled in disputation, in the sixteenth year together with the chariot-commandant Dou Gu and others jointly went out to strike the Xiongnu; afterward those who advanced often envied his ability, and he was repeatedly slandered and accused.
translations.idiomatic: Marquis Zhang of Xiabo was a noted debater; in the sixteenth year he campaigned with Dou Gu and others against the Xiongnu, after which jealous rivals repeatedly impeached him.

sentences.zh: 建初中卒，肅宗下詔褒揚之，復封張子它人奉其祀。

translations.literal: He died in the Jianchu era; the Suzong emperor issued an edict praising him and again enfeoffed a son of Zhang to maintain his sacrifices.
translations.idiomatic: He died during the Jianchu reign; Emperor Zhang issued a decree in his praise and had a son of Zhang re-enfeoffed to continue the family line.

sentences.zh: 論曰：大丈夫之鼓動拔起，其志致蓋遠矣。

translations.literal: The judgment says: When a great man rouses himself and springs up, the aim of his intent and purpose is indeed far-reaching.
translations.idiomatic: The historian’s verdict: when a great man stirs and rises, his designs reach far indeed.

sentences.zh: 若夫齊武王之破家厚士，豈遊俠下客之為哉！

translations.literal: As for Prince Martial of Qi’s ruining his household to enrich shi knights—was that the conduct of wandering swordsmen and low retainers?
translations.idiomatic: Prince Martial of Qi beggared his house to win fighting men—this was no mere knight-errantry.

sentences.zh: 其慮將存乎配天之絕業，而痛明堂之不祀也。

translations.literal: His calculations were about preserving the unmatched enterprise that matches Heaven, and grieving that the Bright Hall went un-sacrificed.
translations.idiomatic: He meant to secure a legacy worthy of Heaven itself, and he mourned that the Bright Hall stood without offerings.

sentences.zh: 及其發舉大謀，在倉卒擾攘之中，使信先成於敵人，赦岑彭以顯義，若此足以見其度矣。

translations.literal: And when he launched his great plan, in the midst of sudden turmoil and confusion, he caused trust to be established first in the enemy, pardoned Cen Peng to display righteousness—such things suffice to show his measure.
translations.idiomatic: Even amid panic and confusion he could bind an enemy’s trust and spare Cen Peng to teach loyalty—acts that reveal the breadth of his mind.

sentences.zh: 誌高慮遠，禍發所忽。

translations.literal: His aims were high and his foresight long, yet disaster sprang from what he overlooked.
translations.idiomatic: He looked far ahead, yet ruin crept in where he never thought to watch.

sentences.zh: 嗚呼！

translations.literal: Alas!
translations.idiomatic: Alas!

sentences.zh: 古人以蜂蠆為戒，蓋畏此也。

translations.literal: The ancients took wasps and scorpions as a warning—surely it was this they feared.
translations.idiomatic: The ancients trembled at wasp and scorpion—this is what they meant.

sentences.zh: 《詩》云：『敬之敬之，命不易哉！』

translations.literal: The Classic of Poetry says, “Revere it, revere it—Heaven’s mandate is not easy.”
translations.idiomatic: As the Odes say: “Be reverent, be reverent—the Mandate of Heaven is not lightly kept.”

sentences.zh: 北海靖王

translations.literal: King Jing of Beihai
translations.idiomatic: King Jing of Beihai

sentences.zh: 北海靖王興，建武二年封為魯王，嗣光武兄仲。

translations.literal: King Jing of Beihai, Xing, in the second year of Jianwu was enfeoffed as king of Lu, succeeding Guangwu’s elder brother Zhong.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xing, King Jing of Beihai, was made king of Lu in Jianwu 2, succeeding Guangwu’s brother Zhong.

sentences.zh: 初，南頓君娶同郡樊重女，字嫻都。

translations.literal: In the beginning the lord of Nandun married a daughter of Fan Chong of the same commandery, styled Xiandu.
translations.idiomatic: Long ago the lord of Nandun had taken as wife the daughter of Fan Chong of the same district, known as Xiandu.

sentences.zh: 嫻都性婉順，自為童女，不正容服不出於房，宗族敬焉。

translations.literal: Xiandu’s nature was gentle and compliant; from the time she was a girl, unless her dress and bearing were correct she did not emerge from the chamber; the lineage respected her.
translations.idiomatic: Lady Xiandu was gentle and modest; even as a girl she would not leave her room unless properly dressed, and the clan honored her for it.

sentences.zh: 生三男三女：長男伯升，次仲，次光武；

translations.literal: She bore three sons and three daughters: the eldest son Bosheng, next Zhong, next Guangwu;
translations.idiomatic: She bore three sons and three daughters: Bosheng, Zhong, and Guangwu;

sentences.zh: 長女黃，次元，次伯姬。

translations.literal: the eldest daughter Huang, next Yuan, next Boji.
translations.idiomatic: the daughters Huang, Yuan, and Boji.

sentences.zh: 皇妣以初起兵時病卒，宗人樊巨公收斂焉。

translations.literal: The august mother died of illness when they first raised troops; a clansman, Fan Jugong, gathered her for burial.
translations.idiomatic: Their mother fell ill and died when the rebellion began; clansman Fan Jugong saw to her burial.

sentences.zh: 建武二年，封黃為湖陽長公主，伯姬為寧平長公主。

translations.literal: In the second year of Jianwu he enfeoffed Huang as senior princess of Huyang and Boji as senior princess of Ningping.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 2 Huang became senior princess of Huyang and Boji senior princess of Ningping.

sentences.zh: 元與仲俱歿於小長安，追爵元為新野長公主，十五年，追謚仲為魯哀王。

translations.literal: Yuan together with Zhong both perished at Little Chang’an; he posthumously ennobled Yuan as senior princess of Xinye; in the fifteenth year he posthumously honored Zhong as King Ai of Lu.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan and Zhong both died at Little Chang’an; Yuan was later titled senior princess of Xinye, and in the fifteenth year Zhong received the posthumous title King Ai of Lu.

sentences.zh: 睦少好學，博通書傳，光武愛之，數被廷納。

translations.literal: Mu in his youth loved learning and broadly comprehended the texts and commentaries; Guangwu loved him and repeatedly received him in the inner court.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Mu loved books from boyhood and mastered the classics; Guangwu doted on him and often summoned him to the palace.

sentences.zh: 顯宗之在東宮，尤見幸待，入侍諷誦，出則執轡。

translations.literal: When the Manifest Ancestor was in the Eastern Palace, he was especially favored and attended; he entered to attend recitation and when going out held the reins.
translations.idiomatic: When the future Emperor Ming was crown prince, Mu was his particular favorite—reading with him indoors and holding the carriage reins outdoors.

sentences.zh: 中興初，禁網尚闊，而睦性謙恭好士，千里交結，自名儒宿德，莫不造門，由是聲價益廣。

translations.literal: At the beginning of the restoration the prohibitions were still loose, yet Mu’s nature was humble and respectful and he loved shi; he formed ties a thousand li away, and from established scholars with old virtue none failed to visit his gate, whereby his reputation and price grew ever broader.
translations.idiomatic: In the early years of the revival the laws were still lax; Mu was modest and a friend to scholars, welcoming famous men from afar until his reputation filled the realm.

sentences.zh: 永平中，法憲頗峻，睦乃謝絕賓客，放心音樂。

translations.literal: In the Yongping period the statutes grew stern, whereupon Mu declined and cut off guests and gave his heart to music.
translations.idiomatic: When Yongping brought stricter rule, he shut his doors to visitors and turned to music for solace.

sentences.zh: 然性好讀書，常為愛玩。

translations.literal: Yet by nature he loved reading and was constantly fond of it as a pastime.
translations.idiomatic: Still, he never lost his love of books; they remained his chief delight.

sentences.zh: 歲終，遣中大夫奉璧朝賀，召而謂之曰：『朝廷設廷問寡人，大夫將何辭以對？

translations.literal: At year’s end he dispatched a palace messenger bearing a jade disc to offer court congratulations; summoning him he said, “The court has set a session to question me, sir; with what words will the grandee reply?”
translations.idiomatic: At year’s end he sent a palace messenger with a jade token to pay court tribute, then called the man in and asked, “When the court examines me in session, what answer will you give?”

sentences.zh: 』使者曰：『大王忠孝慈仁，敬賢樂士。

translations.literal: ” The messenger said, “The great king is loyal, filial, kind, and benevolent; he respects the worthy and delights in knights.”
translations.idiomatic: The envoy replied, “Your Highness is famed for loyalty, filial piety, and love of worthy men.”

sentences.zh: 臣雖螻蟻，敢不以實？

translations.literal: Though your servant is no more than an ant, how dare I not speak the truth?”
translations.idiomatic: I am a mere nobody, sire, yet I would not dare answer with anything but the truth.”

sentences.zh: 』睦曰：『籲，子危我哉！

translations.literal: ” Mu said, “Alas, sir, you would endanger me!”
translations.idiomatic: Mu exclaimed, “You would ruin me with such talk!”

sentences.zh: 此乃孤幼時進趣之行也。

translations.literal: This was conduct of advancing and striving from when I was young and orphaned.”
translations.idiomatic: Those were the rash ambitions of my boyhood.”

sentences.zh: 大夫其對以孤襲爵以來，誌意衰惰，聲色是娛，犬馬是好。

translations.literal: Let the grandee reply on my behalf that since I inherited the title, my aims and intent have declined and become slack, music and women are what I take pleasure in, and dogs and horses are what I love.”
translations.idiomatic: Tell the court that since I came into the title I have lost my edge—that I care for nothing but music, women, dogs, and horses.”

sentences.zh: 』使者受命而行。

translations.literal: ” The messenger received the order and departed.
translations.idiomatic: The messenger took his instructions and left.

sentences.zh: 其能屈申若此。

translations.literal: He was able to bend and extend himself thus.
translations.idiomatic: Such was his skill at bending with the times.

sentences.zh: 初，靖王薨，悉推財產與諸弟，雖王車服珍寶非列侯制，皆以為分，然後隨以金、帛贖之。

translations.literal: In the beginning, when King Jing died, he pushed all property to his various younger brothers; although royal carriage, robes, and precious treasures were not in accord with marquis regulations, he took them all as shares to divide, then afterward followed with gold and silk to redeem them.
translations.idiomatic: When King Jing died, he gave his entire estate to his brothers—even regalia that exceeded a marquis’s rank—then bought back his share in coin and silk.

sentences.zh: 睦能屬文，作《春秋旨義終始論》及賦頌數十篇。

translations.literal: Mu was able to compose texts; he wrote “Discourse on the Meaning and Beginning-to-End of the Spring and Autumn” and several tens of pieces of rhapsodies and eulogies.
translations.idiomatic: He was a fine writer, author of *Discourse on the Spring and Autumn* and dozens of fu and hymns.

sentences.zh: 又善《史書》，當世以為楷則。

translations.literal: He was also skilled in “clerical script”; his contemporaries took it as a model.
translations.idiomatic: He also excelled at clerical calligraphy that contemporaries copied as a standard.

sentences.zh: 及寢病，帝驛馬令作草書尺牘十首。

translations.literal: When he lay gravely ill, the emperor sent post horses ordering him to draft ten pieces of cursive script on slips.
translations.idiomatic: On his deathbed the emperor sent relay riders demanding ten samples of his cursive hand.

sentences.zh: 立十年薨，子哀王基嗣。

translations.literal: Having reigned ten years he died; his son, King Ai, Ji, succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: He ruled ten years and died; his son Liu Ji, posthumously King Ai, inherited.

sentences.zh: 永平十八年，封基二弟為縣侯，二弟為鄉侯。

translations.literal: In the eighteenth year of Yongping he enfeoffed two younger brothers of Ji as county marquises and two younger brothers as village marquises.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongping 18 two of Liu Ji’s brothers were made county marquises and two others village marquises.

sentences.zh: 建初二年，又封基弟毅為平望侯。

translations.literal: In the second year of Jianchu he again enfeoffed Ji’s younger brother Yi as marquis of Pingwang.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianchu 2 another brother, Yi, received the title marquis of Pingwang.

sentences.zh: 基立十四年薨，無子，肅宗憐之，不除其國。

translations.literal: Ji, having reigned fourteen years, died without a son; the Suzong emperor pitied him and did not abolish the kingdom.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Ji died childless after fourteen years; out of compassion Emperor Zhang left the fief in abeyance rather than abolish it.

sentences.zh: 永元二年，和帝封睦庶子斟鄉侯威為北海王，奉睦後。

translations.literal: In the second year of Yongyuan, Emperor He enfeoffed Mu’s bastard son Wei, marquis of Zhenxiang, as king of Beihai to continue Mu’s line.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongyuan 2 Emperor He made Wei, marquis of Zhenxiang and Mu’s natural son, king of Beihai to maintain the sacrifice.

sentences.zh: 立七年，威以非睦子，又坐誹謗，檻車征詣廷尉，道自殺。

translations.literal: Having reigned seven years, because Wei was not Mu’s son and moreover sat guilty of slander, he was summoned in a cage cart to the commandant of justice and killed himself on the road.
translations.idiomatic: Seven years later Wei—who was not truly Mu’s heir—was charged with libel, hauled to the capital in a prison cart, and took his own life en route.

sentences.zh: 永初元年，鄧太后復封睦孫壽光侯普為北海王，是為頃王。

translations.literal: In the first year of Yongchu, Empress Dowager Deng again enfeoffed Pu, marquis of Shouguang and Mu’s grandson, as king of Beihai; this was King Qing.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongchu 1 the Deng regent restored Mu’s grandson Pu, marquis of Shouguang, as king of Beihai—later known as King Qing.

sentences.zh: 延光二年，復封睦少子為亭侯。

translations.literal: In the second year of Yanguang she again enfeoffed one of Mu’s younger sons as a precinct marquis.
translations.idiomatic: In Yanguang 2 she also enfeoffed another of Mu’s younger sons as a precinct marquis.

sentences.zh: 普立十七年薨，子恭王翼嗣；

translations.literal: Pu, having reigned seventeen years, died; his son, King Gong, Yi, succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Pu reigned seventeen years; his son Liu Yi, King Gong, followed him.

sentences.zh: 立十四年薨，子康王嗣，無後，建安十一年，國除。

translations.literal: Having reigned fourteen years he died; his son, King Kang, succeeded; there were no posterity; in the eleventh year of Jian’an the kingdom was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: His successor ruled fourteen years and died without issue, and in Jian’an 11 the kingdom was extinguished.

sentences.zh: 初，臨邑侯復好學，能文章。

translations.literal: In the beginning the marquis of Linyi, Fu, loved learning and was capable at composition.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Fu, marquis of Linyi, was a devoted scholar and a polished writer.

sentences.zh: 永平中，每有講學事，輒令復典掌焉。

translations.literal: In the Yongping period, whenever there were affairs of lecturing and learning, he was always ordered to take charge of them.
translations.idiomatic: During Yongping he was routinely put in charge of court lectures and academies.

sentences.zh: 與班固、賈逵共述漢史，傅毅等皆宗事之。

translations.literal: Together with Ban Gu and Jia Kui he narrated Han history, and Fu Yi and others all took him as their teacher in service.
translations.idiomatic: With Ban Gu and Jia Kui he compiled Han history, while Fu Yi and others looked to him as their patron.

sentences.zh: 復子騊駼及從兄平望侯毅，並有才學。

translations.literal: Fu’s son Taotu and his elder male cousin Yi, marquis of Pingwang, both had talent and learning.
translations.idiomatic: His son Taotu and his cousin Yi, marquis of Pingwang, were likewise gifted scholars.

sentences.zh: 永寧中，鄧太后召毅及騊駼入東觀，與謁者僕射劉珍著中興以下名臣列士傳。

translations.literal: In the Yongning period Empress Dowager Deng summoned Yi and Taotu into the Eastern View, where with the herald superintendent Liu Zhen they wrote biographies of famous ministers and stalwarts from the restoration onward.
translations.idiomatic: Under Yongning the Deng regent brought Yi and Taotu into the Eastern Library to join Liu Zhen in drafting biographies of notable men since the restoration.

sentences.zh: 騊駼又自造賦、頌、書、論凡四篇。

translations.literal: Taotu moreover himself composed in all four pieces of rhapsody, eulogy, letter, and discourse.
translations.idiomatic: Taotu also wrote four original pieces—fu, hymns, letters, and essays.

sentences.zh: 趙孝王

translations.literal: King Xiao of Zhao
translations.idiomatic: King Xiao of Zhao

sentences.zh: 趙孝王良字次伯，光武之叔父也。

translations.literal: King Xiao of Zhao, Liang, styled Cibo, was Guangwu’s paternal uncle.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Liang, King Xiao of Zhao, styled Cibo, was Guangwu’s uncle.

sentences.zh: 平帝時舉孝廉，為蕭令。

translations.literal: In Emperor Ping’s time he was recommended as filial and incorrupt and became magistrate of Xiao.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Ping he entered office as a filial-and-incorrupt nominee and served as magistrate of Xiao.

sentences.zh: 光武兄弟少孤，良撫循甚篤。

translations.literal: Guangwu’s brothers were orphaned while young; Liang cherished and guided them with very deep devotion.
translations.idiomatic: The Liu brothers lost their father early; Liang raised them with steadfast care.

sentences.zh: 及光武起兵，以事告，良大怒，曰：『汝與伯升志操不同，今家欲危亡，而反共謀如是！

translations.literal: When Guangwu raised troops and reported the matter, Liang was greatly angry and said, “You and Bosheng differ in aims and conduct; now the family is about to face peril and ruin, yet you turn and plot together thus!”
translations.idiomatic: When Guangwu announced the revolt, his uncle thundered, “You and Bosheng never agreed in purpose—now the house is in mortal danger, and you would league with him?”

sentences.zh: 』既而不得已，從軍至小長安，漢兵大敗，良妻及二子皆被害。

translations.literal: ” Presently, having no choice, he followed the army to Little Chang’an; the Han troops suffered a great defeat, and Liang’s wife and two sons were all killed in the fighting.
translations.idiomatic: Forced to follow the host to Little Chang’an, he saw the Han army shattered and his wife and two sons slain.

sentences.zh: 更始立，以良為國三老，從入關。

translations.literal: When Gengshi was established, he appointed Liang one of the three elders of the state and followed him in entering the Pass.
translations.idiomatic: Gengshi named him a state elder and took him west through the passes.

sentences.zh: 更始敗。

translations.literal: Gengshi was defeated.
translations.idiomatic: When Gengshi fell,

sentences.zh: 良聞光武即位，乃亡奔洛陽。

translations.literal: Liang heard that Guangwu had ascended the throne, thereupon fled and hastened to Luoyang.
translations.idiomatic: Liang fled to Luoyang upon learning that Guangwu had taken the throne.

sentences.zh: 建武二年，封良為廣陽王。

translations.literal: In the second year of Jianwu he enfeoffed Liang as king of Guangyang.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 2 he was made king of Guangyang.

sentences.zh: 五年，徙為趙王，始就國。

translations.literal: In the fifth year he was moved to be king of Zhao and first went to his kingdom.
translations.idiomatic: In the fifth year he was transferred to Zhao and went to his fief.

sentences.zh: 十三年，降為趙公。

translations.literal: In the thirteenth year he was reduced to duke of Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: In the thirteenth year his rank was cut to duke of Zhao.

sentences.zh: 頻歲來朝。

translations.literal: Year after year he came to court.
translations.idiomatic: He attended court almost every year.

sentences.zh: 十七年，薨於京師。

translations.literal: In the seventeenth year he died at the capital.
translations.idiomatic: He died in the capital in the seventeenth year.

sentences.zh: 凡六十六年。

translations.literal: In all sixty-six years.
translations.idiomatic: He had lived sixty-six years.

sentences.zh: 子節王栩嗣。

translations.literal: His son, King Jie, Xu, succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Xu, King Jie, succeeded him.

sentences.zh: 建武三十年，封栩二子為鄉侯。

translations.literal: In the thirtieth year of Jianwu he enfeoffed two sons of Xu as village marquises.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 30 two of Xu’s sons were enfeoffed as village marquises.

sentences.zh: 建初二年，復封栩十子為亭侯。

translations.literal: In the second year of Jianchu he again enfeoffed ten sons of Xu as precinct marquises.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianchu 2 ten more of Xu’s sons received precinct marquisates.

sentences.zh: 栩立四十年薨，子頃王商嗣。

translations.literal: Xu, having reigned forty years, died; his son, King Qing, Shang, succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Xu ruled forty years; Liu Shang, King Qing, inherited.

sentences.zh: 永元三年，封商三弟為亭侯。

translations.literal: In the third year of Yongyuan he enfeoffed three younger brothers of Shang as precinct marquises.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongyuan 3 three of Shang’s brothers became precinct marquises.

sentences.zh: 元年，封商四子為亭侯。

translations.literal: In the first year of the Yuanchu era he enfeoffed four sons of Shang as precinct marquises.
translations.idiomatic: In Yuanchu 1 four of Shang’s sons were enfeoffed as precinct marquises.

sentences.zh: 商立二十三年薨，子靖王宏嗣。

translations.literal: Shang, having reigned twenty-three years, died; his son, King Jing, Hong, succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Shang died after twenty-three years; Liu Hong, King Jing, followed.

sentences.zh: 立十二年薨，子惠王乾嗣。

translations.literal: Having reigned twelve years he died; his son, King Hui, Qian, succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Hong ruled twelve years; Liu Qian, King Hui, succeeded.

sentences.zh: 元初五年，封乾二弟為亭侯。

translations.literal: In the fifth year of Yuanchu he enfeoffed two younger brothers of Qian as precinct marquises.
translations.idiomatic: In Yuanchu 5 two of Qian’s brothers received precinct titles.

sentences.zh: 是歲，趙相奏乾居父喪私娉小妻，又白衣出司馬門，坐削中丘縣。

translations.literal: That year the chancellor of Zhao reported that during mourning for his father Qian had privately taken a minor wife and had gone out through the Sima Gate in plain dress; he sat guilty and Zhongqiu county was stripped away.
translations.idiomatic: The same year Zhao’s chancellor impeached Qian for marrying a concubine during his father’s mourning and passing the palace gate in undress; he was fined the income of Zhongqiu county.

sentences.zh: 時郎中南陽程堅素有誌行，拜為乾傅。

translations.literal: At the time Cheng Jian of Nanyang, gentleman of the palace, had long had repute for purpose and conduct, and was appointed tutor to Qian.
translations.idiomatic: The court then named Cheng Jian of Nanyang, a man of proven integrity, as his tutor.

sentences.zh: 堅輔以禮義，乾改悔前過，堅列上，復所削縣。

translations.literal: Jian assisted him with ritual and righteousness; Qian reformed and repented of former faults; Jian listed and submitted it upward, and the stripped county was restored.
translations.idiomatic: Jian guided him with ritual until he mended his ways; a memorial to the throne won back the lost district.

sentences.zh: 本初元年，封乾一子為亭侯。

translations.literal: In the first year of Benchu he enfeoffed one son of Qian as a precinct marquis.
translations.idiomatic: In Benchu 1 one of Qian’s sons received a precinct marquisate.

sentences.zh: 乾立四十八年薨，子懷王豫嗣。

translations.literal: Qian, having reigned forty-eight years, died; his son, King Huai, Yu, succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Qian ruled forty-eight years; Liu Yu, King Huai, inherited.

sentences.zh: 豫薨，子獻王赦嗣。

translations.literal: When Yu died, his son, King Xian, She, succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Yu was followed by Liu She, King Xian.

sentences.zh: 赦薨，子珪嗣，建安十八年徙封博陵王。

translations.literal: When She died, his son Gui succeeded; in the eighteenth year of Jian’an he was moved and enfeoffed as king of Boling.
translations.idiomatic: Liu She’s son Liu Gui succeeded him, and in Jian’an 18 was transferred to the kingdom of Boling.

sentences.zh: 立九年，魏初以為崇德侯。

translations.literal: Having reigned nine years, at the beginning of Wei he was made marquis of Chongde.
translations.idiomatic: Nine years later, at the Wei founding, he was given the title marquis of Chongde.

sentences.zh: 城陽恭王

translations.literal: King Gong of Chengyang
translations.idiomatic: King Gong of Chengyang

sentences.zh: 城陽恭王祉字巨伯，光武族兄、舂陵康侯敞之子也。

translations.literal: King Gong of Chengyang, Zhi, styled Jubo, was a cousin elder brother of Guangwu and the son of Marquis Kang of Chunling, Chang.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Zhi, King Gong of Chengyang, styled Jubo, was Guangwu’s elder clansman and son of Liu Chang, Marquis Kang of Chunling.

sentences.zh: 敞曾祖父節侯買，以長沙定王子封於零道之舂陵鄉，為舂陵侯。

translations.literal: Chang’s great-grandfather, Marquis Jie, Mai, because he was a son of the prince who fixed Changsha was enfeoffed at Chunling township in Lingdao and became marquis of Chunling.
translations.idiomatic: His great-grandfather Mai, Marquis Jie, was a son of the prince who stabilized Changsha and received Chunling township in Lingdao.

sentences.zh: 買卒，子戴侯熊渠嗣。

translations.literal: When Mai died, his son, Marquis Dai, Xiongqu, succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Mai’s son Xiongqu, Marquis Dai, succeeded him.

sentences.zh: 熊渠卒，子考侯仁嗣。

translations.literal: When Xiongqu died, his son, Marquis Kao, Ren, succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Xiongqu was followed by Ren, Marquis Kao.

sentences.zh: 仁以舂陵地勢下濕，山林毒氣，上書求減邑內徙。

translations.literal: Ren, because Chunling’s terrain was low and damp and the mountain forests had poisonous vapors, submitted a memorial requesting reduction of the domain and removal inward.
translations.idiomatic: Ren petitioned to move the seat inland, citing Chunling’s malarial lowlands and poisonous woods.

sentences.zh: 元帝初元四年，徙封南陽之白水鄉，猶以舂陵為國名，遂與從弟巨鹿都尉回及宗族往家焉。

translations.literal: In the fourth year of Chuyuan of Emperor Yuan he was moved and enfeoffed at Baishui township in Nanyang, still taking Chunling as the kingdom name; thereupon together with his younger male cousin Hui, commandant of Julu, and the lineage group he went and made a home there.
translations.idiomatic: In Yuan’s Chuyuan 4 the fief was relocated to Baishui in Nanyang while keeping the name Chunling; Ren led his cousin Hui, commandant of Julu, and the whole clan thither.

sentences.zh: 仁卒，子敞嗣。

translations.literal: When Ren died, his son Chang succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Ren’s heir was Chang.

sentences.zh: 敞謙儉好義，盡推父時金寶財產與昆弟，刑州刺史上其義行，拜廬江都尉。

translations.literal: Chang was humble, frugal, and loved righteousness; he pushed all the gold, treasures, and property from his father’s time to his brothers; the provincial governor of Jing submitted upward his righteous conduct, and he was appointed commandant of Lujiang.
translations.idiomatic: Chang was modest and generous, handing his father’s wealth to his brothers; the governor of Jingzhou praised his virtue and named him commandant of Lujiang.

sentences.zh: 歲餘，會族兄安眾侯劉崇起兵，王莽畏惡劉氏，征敞至長安，免歸國。

translations.literal: After more than a year it happened that his lineage elder, Marquis Anzhong, Liu Chong, raised troops; Wang Mang feared and hated the Liu clan and summoned Chang to Chang’an, dismissed him, and sent him back to the kingdom.
translations.idiomatic: A year later kinsman Liu Chong of Anzhong rose in revolt; Wang Mang, fearing the imperial surname, recalled Chang to Chang’an, dismissed him, and sent him home.

sentences.zh: 先是平帝時，敞與崇俱朝京師，助祭明堂。

translations.literal: Earlier, in Emperor Ping’s time, Chang together with Chong both had audience at the capital and assisted in sacrifice at the Bright Hall.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier, under Emperor Ping, Chang and Chong had both attended court and helped sacrifice at the Bright Hall.

sentences.zh: 崇見莽將危漢室，私謂敞曰：『安漢公擅國權，群臣莫不回從，社稷傾覆至矣。

translations.literal: Chong saw that Mang was about to endanger the Han house and privately said to Chang, “The Lord Who Pacifies the Han monopolizes state power; the host of ministers none fail to turn and follow; the altars of soil and grain are toppling to the limit.
translations.idiomatic: Chong warned him in private, “The Lord Who Pacifies the Han holds every rein; the court bows in lockstep—the dynasty is tottering.

sentences.zh: 太后春秋高，天子幼弱，高皇帝所以分封子弟，蓋為此也。

translations.literal: The empress dowager’s years are advanced; the Son of Heaven is young and weak; the reason the High Ancestor enfeoffed sons and younger brothers was surely for this.”
translations.idiomatic: The Grand Empress is old, the boy emperor helpless—this is why Gaozu scattered fiefs among his kin.”

sentences.zh: 』敞心然之。

translations.literal: ” Chang’s heart approved it.
translations.idiomatic: Chang knew he spoke the truth.

sentences.zh: 及崇事敗，敞懼，欲結援樹黨，乃為祉娶高陵侯翟宣女為妻。

translations.literal: When Chong’s affair failed, Chang was afraid and wished to knot support and plant a faction; thereupon for Zhi he took as wife the daughter of Marquis Gaoling, Zhai Xuan.
translations.idiomatic: After Chong’s plot collapsed, Chang sought allies by marrying Zhi to a daughter of Zhai Xuan, marquis of Gaoling.

sentences.zh: 會宣弟義起兵欲攻莽，南陽捕殺宣女，祉坐繫獄。

translations.literal: It happened that Xuan’s younger brother Yi raised troops intending to attack Mang; Nanyang seized and killed Xuan’s daughter, and Zhi sat guilty and was imprisoned.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhai Yi rose against Wang Mang, Nanyang executed Xuan’s daughter, and Zhi was jailed as an accomplice.

sentences.zh: 敞因上書謝罪，願率子弟宗族為士卒先。

translations.literal: Chang thereupon submitted a memorial confessing guilt, willing to lead sons and younger brothers and the lineage group as vanguard of the soldiers.
translations.idiomatic: Chang memorialized in abject terms, offering to lead his kin as shock troops.

sentences.zh: 莽新居攝，欲慰安宗室，故不被刑誅。

translations.literal: Wang Mang had newly taken the regency and wished to console and reassure the imperial clan; therefore he was not subjected to punishment and execution.
translations.idiomatic: Mang, fresh in the regency and eager to placate the Liu house, spared him execution.

sentences.zh: 及莽篡立，劉氏為侯者皆降稱子，食孤卿祿，後皆奪爵。

translations.literal: When Mang usurped the throne, those of the Liu clan who were marquises were all reduced in title to be called “sons,” ate the salary of a solitary minister, and afterward all had their nobility stripped.
translations.idiomatic: At Mang’s usurpation Liu marquises were demoted to the empty title “son” on a minister’s stipend, then stripped entirely.

sentences.zh: 及敞卒，祉遂特見廢，又不得官為吏。

translations.literal: When Chang died, Zhi thereupon alone was seen as cast aside and moreover could not obtain office as an official.
translations.idiomatic: After Chang’s death Zhi alone was left in disgrace and barred from office.

sentences.zh: 祉以故侯嫡子，行淳厚，宗室皆敬之。

translations.literal: Zhi, as legitimate son of a former marquis, conducted himself pure and thick; the imperial clan all respected him.
translations.idiomatic: Yet as the old marquis’s heir he lived plainly and honorably, and the clan revered him.

sentences.zh: 及光武起兵，祉兄弟相率從軍，前隊大夫甄阜盡收其家屬繫宛獄。

translations.literal: When Guangwu raised troops, Zhi and his brothers together led one another in following the army; the front-rank grandee Zhen Fu seized all their family dependents and imprisoned them in the jail at Wan.
translations.idiomatic: When Guangwu rose, Zhi and his brothers joined the host; Zhen Fu arrested their families and locked them in Wan’s prison.

sentences.zh: 及漢兵敗小長安，祉挺身還保棘陽，甄阜盡殺其母弟妻子。

translations.literal: When the Han troops were defeated at Little Chang’an, Zhi alone slipped through and returned to hold Jiyang; Zhen Fu killed all his mother, younger brothers, wife, and children in full.
translations.idiomatic: After the rout at Little Chang’an Zhi fought his way back to Jiyang while Fu slaughtered his mother, brothers, wife, and children.

sentences.zh: 更始立，以祉為太常將軍，紹封舂陵侯。

translations.literal: When Gengshi was established, he appointed Zhi general of the grand invocator and renewed the enfeoffment as marquis of Chunling.
translations.idiomatic: Gengshi named him grand invocator general and restored the Chunling marquisate.

sentences.zh: 從西入關，封為定陶王。

translations.literal: Following west into the Pass, he was enfeoffed as king of Dingtao.
translations.idiomatic: He marched west and received the kingdom of Dingtao.

sentences.zh: 別將擊破劉嬰於臨涇。

translations.literal: A separate column broke Liu Ying at Linjing.
translations.idiomatic: A detached force crushed Liu Ying at Linjing.

sentences.zh: 及更始降於赤眉，祉乃間行亡奔洛陽。

translations.literal: When Gengshi surrendered to the Red Eyebrows, Zhi thereupon by stealthy routes fled and hastened to Luoyang.
translations.idiomatic: When Gengshi yielded to the Red Eyebrows, Zhi stole away to Luoyang.

sentences.zh: 是時宗室唯祉先至，光武見之歡甚。

translations.literal: At that time among the imperial clan only Zhi had arrived first; Guangwu, seeing him, was greatly pleased.
translations.idiomatic: He was the first Liu kinsman to reach the new emperor, who welcomed him joyfully.

sentences.zh: 建武二年，封為城陽王，賜乘輿、禦物、車馬、衣服。

translations.literal: In the second year of Jianwu he was enfeoffed as king of Chengyang and was granted imperial carriage gear, regalia, chariots, horses, and robes.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 2 he became king of Chengyang with gifts of the imperial equipage.

sentences.zh: 追謚敞為康侯。

translations.literal: He posthumously honored Chang as Marquis Kang.
translations.idiomatic: Chang was posthumously titled Marquis Kang.

sentences.zh: 十一年，祉疾病，上城陽王璽綬，願以列侯奉先人祭祀。

translations.literal: In the eleventh year Zhi was ill with disease; he offered up the king of Chengyang’s seals and ribbons, wishing by rank of full marquis to attend the sacrifices to his ancestors.
translations.idiomatic: In year 11, mortally ill, he returned the royal seals, asking to end his days as a full marquis so he could tend the family shrines.

sentences.zh: 帝自臨其疾。

translations.literal: The emperor personally attended his illness.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor visited his sickbed in person.

sentences.zh: 祉薨，年四十三，謚曰恭王，竟不之國，葬於洛陽北芒。

translations.literal: Zhi died at age forty-three; his posthumous title was King Gong; in the end he did not go to the kingdom and was buried on Mang Hill north of Luoyang.
translations.idiomatic: He died at forty-three with the posthumous name Gong, never having gone to his fief; he lies buried on the Mang plateau north of Luoyang.

sentences.zh: 十三年，封祉嫡子平為蔡陽侯，以奉祉祀；

translations.literal: In the thirteenth year he enfeoffed Zhi’s legitimate son Ping as marquis of Caiyang to attend Zhi’s sacrifices;
translations.idiomatic: In the thirteenth year his heir Ping was made marquis of Caiyang to maintain his father’s cult;

sentences.zh: 平弟堅為高鄉侯。

translations.literal: Ping’s younger brother Jian was marquis of Gaoxiang.
translations.idiomatic: while a younger brother, Jian, received Gaoxiang.

sentences.zh: 初，建武二年，以皇祖、皇考墓為昌陵，置陵令守視；

translations.literal: In the beginning, in the second year of Jianwu, he took the tombs of the imperial grandfather and imperial father as Changling and established a tomb overseer to guard and watch them;
translations.idiomatic: Early in Jianwu 2 the tombs of Guangwu’s grandfather and father were named Changling and placed under a tomb warden;

sentences.zh: 後改為章陵，因以舂陵為章陵縣。

translations.literal: Afterward it was changed to Zhangling, and thereby Chunling became Zhangling county.
translations.idiomatic: they were later renamed Zhangling, and Chunling county was renamed to match.

sentences.zh: 十八年，立考侯、康侯廟，比園陵，置嗇夫。

translations.literal: In the eighteenth year he established temples to Marquis Kao and Marquis Kang, comparable to the park tombs, and established a bailiff.
translations.idiomatic: In year 18 temples to Marquis Kao and Marquis Kang were built beside the mausoleum parks with a bailiff appointed.

sentences.zh: 詔零陵郡奉祠節侯、戴侯廟，以四時及臘歲五祠焉。

translations.literal: He ordered Lingling commandery to offer sacrifice at the temples to Marquis Jie and Marquis Dai, with five sacrifices in the four seasons and at the year-end la.
translations.idiomatic: Lingling was charged with seasonal and La-day rites at the shrines of Marquis Jie and Marquis Dai.

sentences.zh: 置嗇夫、佐吏各一人。

translations.literal: He established one bailiff and one assistant clerk each.
translations.idiomatic: Each site received a bailiff and an assistant clerk.

sentences.zh: 平後坐與諸王交通，國除。

translations.literal: Later Ping sat guilty of trafficking with the various kings and the kingdom was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: Ping later lost his title for illicit dealings with other princes.

sentences.zh: 永平五年，顯宗更封平為竟陵侯。

translations.literal: In the fifth year of Yongping the Manifest Ancestor changed the enfeoffment of Ping to be marquis of Jingling.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongping 5 Emperor Ming transferred Ping to the marquisate of Jingling.

sentences.zh: 平卒，子真嗣。

translations.literal: When Ping died, his son Zhen succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Ping was succeeded by his son Zhen.

sentences.zh: 真卒，子禹嗣。

translations.literal: When Zhen died, his son Yu succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Zhen was followed by Yu.

sentences.zh: 禹卒，子嘉嗣。

translations.literal: When Yu died, his son Jia succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Yu was followed by Jia.

sentences.zh: 泗水王

translations.literal: King of Sishui
translations.idiomatic: King of Sishui

sentences.zh: 泗水王歙字經孫，光武族父也。

translations.literal: The king of Sishui, Xi, styled Jingsun, was a paternal uncle by clan of Guangwu.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xi, king of Sishui, styled Jingsun, was Guangwu’s kinsman of the elder generation.

sentences.zh: 歙子終，與光武少相親愛。

translations.literal: Xi’s son Zhong in youth was mutually intimate and dear with Guangwu.
translations.idiomatic: His son Zhong had been Guangwu’s close companion since boyhood.

sentences.zh: 漢兵起，始及唐子，終誘殺湖陽尉。

translations.literal: When the Han troops rose, as soon as they reached Tangzi, Zhong enticed and killed the commandant of Huyang.
translations.idiomatic: At the first rising, when the army reached Tangzi, Zhong lured out and slew the Huyang district officer.

sentences.zh: 更始立，歙從入關，封為元氏王，終為侍中。

translations.literal: When Gengshi was established, Xi followed in entering the Pass and was enfeoffed as king of Yuanshi, and Zhong became palace attendant.
translations.idiomatic: Under Gengshi, Xi entered the passes as king of Yuanshi while Zhong served as palace attendant.

sentences.zh: 更始敗，歙、終東奔洛陽。

translations.literal: When Gengshi was defeated, Xi and Zhong fled east to Luoyang.
translations.idiomatic: After Gengshi’s fall they raced east to Luoyang.

sentences.zh: 建武二年，立歙為泗水王，終為淄川王。

translations.literal: In the second year of Jianwu he established Xi as king of Sishui and Zhong as king of Zichuan.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 2 Xi became king of Sishui and Zhong king of Zichuan.

sentences.zh: 十年，歙薨，封小子燀為堂谿侯，奉歙後。

translations.literal: In the tenth year Xi died; he enfeoffed the youngest son Shan as marquis of Tangxi to continue Xi’s line.
translations.idiomatic: Xi died in year 10; his youngest son Shan received Tangxi to maintain his father’s sacrifice.

sentences.zh: 終居喪思慕，哭泣二十八日，亦薨。

translations.literal: Zhong dwelt in mourning, longing in thought, weeping twenty-eight days, and also died.
translations.idiomatic: Zhong mourned so bitterly that he wept twenty-eight days straight and followed his father to the grave.

sentences.zh: 封長子柱為己阝侯，以奉終祀，又封終子鳳曲陽侯。

translations.literal: He enfeoffed the eldest son Zhu as marquis of Jiyi to attend Zhong’s sacrifices, and also enfeoffed Zhong’s son Feng as marquis of Quyang.
translations.idiomatic: The eldest son Zhu was made marquis of Jiyi to keep Zhong’s rites, while Zhong’s son Feng received Quyang.

sentences.zh: 歙從父弟茂，年十八，漢兵之起，茂自號劉失職，亦聚眾亦、密間，稱厭新將軍。

translations.literal: Xi’s younger male cousin by the same grandfather, Mao, age eighteen—when the Han troops rose, Mao styled himself “Liu who has lost office,” also gathered a multitude in the borderlands of Yi and Mi, and called himself general who crushes the New.
translations.idiomatic: Xi’s cousin Mao, only eighteen, styled himself a dispossessed Liu, raised ten thousand followers between Yi and Mi, and took the title “General Who Crushes the New.”

sentences.zh: 攻下潁川、汝南，眾十餘萬人。

translations.literal: He attacked and took Yingchuan and Runan; the multitude was more than a hundred thousand.
translations.idiomatic: He overran Yingchuan and Runan until his host topped a hundred thousand.

sentences.zh: 光武既至河內，茂率眾降，封為中山王。

translations.literal: After Guangwu had reached Henei, Mao led the multitude in submission and was enfeoffed as king of Zhongshan.
translations.idiomatic: When Guangwu entered Henei, Mao surrendered with his army and was named king of Zhongshan.

sentences.zh: 十三年，宗室為王者皆降為侯，更封茂為穰侯。

translations.literal: In the thirteenth year those of the imperial clan who were kings were all reduced to marquises; Mao was again enfeoffed as marquis of Rang.
translations.idiomatic: In year 13 princely titles were cut to marquisates; Mao became marquis of Rang.

sentences.zh: 茂弟匡，亦與漢兵俱起。

translations.literal: Mao’s younger brother Kuang likewise rose with the Han troops.
translations.idiomatic: Kuang, Mao’s brother, had also joined the Han uprising.

sentences.zh: 建武二年，封宜春侯。

translations.literal: In the second year of Jianwu he was enfeoffed as marquis of Yichun.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 2 he received Yichun.

sentences.zh: 為人謙遜，永平中為宗正。

translations.literal: By nature he was modest and yielding; in the Yongping period he became director of the imperial clan.
translations.idiomatic: A humble man, he later served as director of the imperial clan under Yongping.

sentences.zh: 子浮嗣，封朝陽侯。

translations.literal: His son Fu succeeded and was enfeoffed as marquis of Zhaoyang.
translations.idiomatic: His son Fu inherited and was transferred to Zhaoyang.

sentences.zh: 浮弟尚，永元中為征西將軍。

translations.literal: Fu’s younger brother Shang in the Yongyuan period became general on the western expedition.
translations.idiomatic: Fu’s brother Shang rose to western-expedition general in the Yongyuan era.

sentences.zh: 浮傳國至孫護，無子，封絕。

translations.literal: The state was transmitted to Fu’s grandson Hu; having no son, the enfeoffment was cut off.
translations.idiomatic: The line ran to Fu’s grandson Hu, who died without heirs, and the title lapsed.

sentences.zh: 延光中，護從兄瑰與安帝乳母王聖女伯榮私通，遂取伯榮為妻，得紹護封為朝陽侯，位侍中。

translations.literal: In the Yanguang period Hu’s elder male cousin Gui had illicit relations with Boren, daughter of Emperor An’s wet nurse Wang Sheng; thereupon he took Boren as wife, obtained renewal of Hu’s enfeoffment as marquis of Zhaoyang, and held rank as palace attendant.
translations.idiomatic: During Yanguang, Hu’s cousin Gui wed Boren—wet nurse Wang’s daughter—and so revived the Zhaoyang marquisate, rising to palace attendant.

sentences.zh: 及王聖敗，貶爵為亭侯。

translations.literal: When Wang Sheng fell, his nobility was demoted to precinct marquis.
translations.idiomatic: When Wang Sheng fell, Gui was demoted to a precinct marquis.

sentences.zh: 安城孝侯

translations.literal: Marquis Xiao of Ancheng
translations.idiomatic: Marquis Xiao of Ancheng

sentences.zh: 安城孝侯賜字子琴，光武族兄也。

translations.literal: Marquis Xiao of Ancheng, Ci, styled Ziqin, was an elder male cousin of Guangwu.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Ci, Marquis Xiao of Ancheng, styled Ziqin, was Guangwu’s elder clansman.

sentences.zh: 祖父利，蒼梧太守。

translations.literal: His grandfather Li was governor of Cangwu.
translations.idiomatic: His grandfather Li had governed Cangwu.

sentences.zh: 賜少孤。

translations.literal: Ci was orphaned while young.
translations.idiomatic: Ci lost his father early.

sentences.zh: 兄顯報怨殺人，吏捕顯殺之。

translations.literal: His elder brother Xian killed a man in revenge for a grudge; clerks seized Xian and executed him.
translations.idiomatic: His brother Xian slew a foe in a blood feud and was caught and put to death.

sentences.zh: 賜與顯子信賣田宅，同拋財產，結客報吏，皆亡命逃伏遭赦日。

translations.literal: Ci together with Xian’s son Xin sold fields and houses, together cast away property, associated with clients to strike at the clerks, and both fled their lives and hid until the day of amnesty.
translations.idiomatic: Ci and Xian’s son Xin sold the estate, spent every coin hiring swordsmen against the magistrates, and lived as outlaws until amnesty.

sentences.zh: 會伯升起兵，乃隨從攻擊諸縣。

translations.literal: It happened that Bosheng raised troops; thereupon he followed in attacking the various counties.
translations.idiomatic: When Bosheng rose, he joined the campaign across the commanderies.

sentences.zh: 更始既立，以賜為光祿勛，封廣漢侯。

translations.literal: After Gengshi was already established, he appointed Ci superintendent of the imperial household and enfeoffed him as marquis of Guanghan.
translations.idiomatic: Gengshi named him superintendent of the imperial household and marquis of Guanghan.

sentences.zh: 及伯升被害，代為大司徒，將兵討汝南。

translations.literal: When Bosheng was murdered, he replaced him as grand minister over the masses and led troops to punish Runan.
translations.idiomatic: After Bosheng’s murder he became grand minister over the masses and marched on Runan.

sentences.zh: 未及平，更始又以信為奮威大將軍，代賜擊汝南，賜與更始俱到洛陽。

translations.literal: Before it was pacified, Gengshi again appointed Xin great general who rouses might, replacing Ci to strike Runan, and Ci together with Gengshi both arrived at Luoyang.
translations.idiomatic: Before the commandery fell, Gengshi sent Xin as “rousing might” general in his place while Ci accompanied the court to Luoyang.

sentences.zh: 更始欲令親近大將徇河北，未知所使，賜言諸家子獨有文叔可用，大司馬朱鮪等以為不可，更始狐疑，賜深勸之，乃拜光武行大司馬，持節過河。

translations.literal: Gengshi wished to send a great general who was intimate to patrol north of the Yellow River and did not know whom to dispatch; Ci said that among the various house sons only Wen Shu could be used; the grand marshal Zhu Wei and others deemed it unacceptable; Gengshi hesitated; Ci urged him deeply, and thereupon he appointed Guangwu acting grand marshal, bearing credentials to cross the river.
translations.idiomatic: Gengshi needed a favorite general for Hebei; Ci insisted only Liu Xiu was fit—Zhu Wei objected—but Ci argued until Gengshi named Guangwu acting grand marshal with staff to cross the Yellow River.

sentences.zh: 是日以賜為丞相，令先入關，修宗廟宮室。

translations.literal: That day he appointed Ci chancellor and ordered him to enter the Pass first and repair the ancestral temples and palace chambers.
translations.idiomatic: The same day Ci became chancellor, ordered west ahead of the army to restore the shrines and palaces.

sentences.zh: 還迎更始都長安，封賜為宛王，拜前大司馬，使持節鎮撫關東。

translations.literal: Returning, he welcomed Gengshi to make his capital at Chang’an, enfeoffed Ci as king of Wan, appointed him former grand marshal, and sent him bearing credentials to pacify and soothe east of the Pass.
translations.idiomatic: He then escorted Gengshi to Chang’an, made Ci king of Wan and ex–grand marshal, and charged him with the lands east of the pass.

sentences.zh: 二年春，賜就國於宛，典將六部兵。

translations.literal: In the second spring Ci went to his kingdom at Wan, in charge of six-column troops.
translations.idiomatic: In the second spring he took up his fief at Wan commanding six divisions.

sentences.zh: 後赤眉破更始，賜所領六部亦稍散畔，乃去宛保育陽。

translations.literal: Afterward the Red Eyebrows broke Gengshi; the six columns that Ci led also gradually scattered and rebelled; thereupon he left Wan and nursed and protected Wuyang.
translations.idiomatic: When the Red Eyebrows destroyed Gengshi, his six corps melted away; he abandoned Wan and shielded Wuyang.

sentences.zh: 聞光武即位，乃西之武關，迎更始妻子將詣洛陽。

translations.literal: Hearing that Guangwu had ascended the throne, he thereupon went west to Wuguan, welcomed Gengshi’s wife and children, and was about to bring them to Luoyang.
translations.idiomatic: Learning of Guangwu’s accession, he slipped to Wuguan, gathered Gengshi’s family, and set out for Luoyang.

sentences.zh: 帝嘉賜忠，建武二年，封為慎侯。

translations.literal: The emperor praised Ci’s loyalty; in the second year of Jianwu he enfeoffed him as marquis of Shen.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor hailed his loyalty and in Jianwu 2 made him marquis of Shen.

sentences.zh: 十三年，更增戶邑，定封為安成侯，奉朝請。

translations.literal: In the thirteenth year he further increased households and territory and fixed the enfeoffment as marquis of Ancheng, with privilege of court audience on summons.
translations.idiomatic: In year 13 his fief was enlarged to Ancheng with standing summons to court.

sentences.zh: 以賜有恩信，故親厚之，數蒙宴私，時幸其第，恩賜特異。

translations.literal: Because Ci had favor and good faith, therefore he was intimate and treated him thickly, repeatedly received him at private banquets, from time to time visited his residence, and favors and gifts were especially unusual.
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu loved him for old debts of trust—private feasts, imperial visits, and gifts beyond the common run.

sentences.zh: 賜輒賑與故舊，有無遺積。

translations.literal: Ci always gave relief to old friends and had nothing left in accumulation.
translations.idiomatic: He poured his income into helping old friends and died without a hoard.

sentences.zh: 帝為營冢堂，起祠廟，置吏卒，如舂陵孝侯。

translations.literal: The emperor built a tomb hall for him, raised a shrine, established clerks and soldiers, as for Marquis Xiao of Chunling.
translations.idiomatic: The court built him a mortuary chapel and shrine guard like that of Marquis Xiao of Chunling.

sentences.zh: 二十八年卒，子閔嗣。

translations.literal: In the twenty-eighth year he died; his son Min succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: He died in year 28; his son Min inherited.

sentences.zh: 三十年，帝復封閔弟嵩為白牛侯。

translations.literal: In the thirtieth year the emperor again enfeoffed Min’s younger brother Song as marquis of Bainiu.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 30 Min’s brother Song received Bainiu.

sentences.zh: 坐楚事，辭語相連，國除。

translations.literal: He sat guilty in the Chu affair; words in testimony linked together, and the kingdom was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: Implicated in the Chu conspiracy, he lost his title.

sentences.zh: 閔卒，子商嗣，徙封為白牛侯。

translations.literal: When Min died, his son Shang succeeded, moved the enfeoffment, and became marquis of Bainiu.
translations.idiomatic: Min’s heir Shang was shifted to the Bainiu marquisate.

sentences.zh: 商卒，子昌嗣。

translations.literal: When Shang died, his son Chang succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Shang was followed by Chang.

sentences.zh: 成武孝侯

translations.literal: Marquis Xiao of Chengwu
translations.idiomatic: Marquis Xiao of Chengwu

sentences.zh: 成武孝侯順字平仲，光武族兄也。

translations.literal: Marquis Xiao of Chengwu, Shun, styled Pingzhong, was an elder male cousin of Guangwu.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Shun, Marquis Xiao of Chengwu, styled Pingzhong, was another elder clansman of Guangwu.

sentences.zh: 父慶，舂陵侯敞同產弟。

translations.literal: His father Qing was a full younger brother of the same birth as Marquis Chang of Chunling.
translations.idiomatic: His father Qing was a full brother of Marquis Chang of Chunling.

sentences.zh: 順與光武同里闬，少相厚。

translations.literal: Shun and Guangwu were of the same village lanes; in youth they were mutually thick.
translations.idiomatic: He and Guangwu had grown up in the same ward and were close from boyhood.

sentences.zh: 初，順叔父弘娶於樊氏，皇妣之從妹也。

translations.literal: In the beginning Shun’s paternal uncle Hong married into the Fan clan; she was a younger female cousin of the august mother.
translations.idiomatic: His uncle Hong had married a cousin of Guangwu’s mother.

sentences.zh: 生二子：敏，國。

translations.literal: She bore two sons: Min and Guo.
translations.idiomatic: The union produced Min and Guo.

sentences.zh: 與母隨更始在長安。

translations.literal: Together with their mother they followed Gengshi at Chang’an.
translations.idiomatic: Mother and sons followed Gengshi in Chang’an.

sentences.zh: 建武二年，詣洛陽，光武封敏為甘裏侯，國為弋陽侯。

translations.literal: In the second year of Jianwu they went to Luoyang; Guangwu enfeoffed Min as marquis of Ganli and Guo as marquis of Yiyang.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 2 they reached Luoyang; Min became marquis of Ganli and Guo marquis of Yiyang.

sentences.zh: 敏通經有行，永平初，官至越騎校尉。

translations.literal: Min comprehended the classics and had conduct; at the beginning of Yongping he reached office as colonel of agile cavalry.
translations.idiomatic: Min was a classicist of good repute and rose to colonel of agile cavalry early in Yongping.

sentences.zh: 弘弟梁，以俠氣聞，更始元年，起兵豫章，欲徇江東，自號『就漢大將軍』，暴病卒。

translations.literal: Hong’s younger brother Liang, famed for knight-errant spirit, in the first year of Gengshi raised troops in Yuzhang, intending to take the lands east of the Yangzi, styled himself “Great General Who Draws Nigh to the Han,” and died suddenly of illness.
translations.idiomatic: Liang, Hong’s brother famed for bravado, raised Yuzhang in Gengshi 1, styled himself “Great General Who Draws Nigh to the Han,” and aimed at the southeast—then died of a sudden illness.

sentences.zh: 順陽懷侯

translations.literal: Marquis Huai of Shunyang
translations.idiomatic: Marquis Huai of Shunyang

sentences.zh: 順陽懷侯嘉字孝孫，光武族兄也。

translations.literal: Marquis Huai of Shunyang, Jia, styled Xiaosun, was an elder male cousin of Guangwu.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Jia, Marquis Huai of Shunyang, styled Xiaosun, was Guangwu’s elder clansman.

sentences.zh: 父憲，舂陵侯敞同產弟。

translations.literal: His father Xian was a full younger brother of the same birth as Marquis Chang of Chunling.
translations.idiomatic: His father Xian was another full brother of Marquis Chang of Chunling.

sentences.zh: 嘉少孤，性仁厚，南頓君養視如子，後與伯升俱學長安，習《尚書》、《春秋》。

translations.literal: Jia was orphaned while young; by nature he was kind and thick; the lord of Nandun reared and looked after him as a son; afterward he together with Bosheng both studied at Chang’an, practicing the “Exalted Document” and the “Spring and Autumn.”
translations.idiomatic: Orphaned young, Jia was raised like a son by the lord of Nandun; he later read the *Documents* and *Spring and Autumn* with Bosheng in Chang’an.

sentences.zh: 李寶等聞鄧禹西征，擁兵自守，馬嘉且觀成敗。

translations.literal: Li Bao and the others, hearing that Deng Yu was on a western expedition, held their troops on the defensive; Liu Jia for the time being watched to see how success or failure would turn.
translations.idiomatic: When Li Bao and his allies learned that Deng Yu was marching west, they stood on the defensive while Liu Jia waited to see which way the wind would blow.

sentences.zh: 光武聞之，告禹曰：『孝孫素謹善，少且親愛，當是長安輕薄兒誤之耳。

translations.literal: Guangwu heard of it and told Yu, saying, “Xiaosun has always been careful and good; in youth moreover he was intimate and dear; surely it is the frivolous youths of Chang’an who misled him.”
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu said to Deng Yu, “Jia has always been steady—some Chang’an wastrels must have led him astray.”

sentences.zh: 』禹即宣帝旨，嘉乃因來歙詣禹於雲陽。

translations.literal: ” Yu thereupon proclaimed the emperor’s intent, and Jia thereupon through Lai Xi went to Yu at Yunyang.
translations.idiomatic: Deng Yu relayed the imperial word, and Jia came in through Lai Xi to surrender at Yunyang.

sentences.zh: 三年，到洛陽，從征伐，拜為千乘太守。

translations.literal: In the third year he arrived at Luoyang, followed in campaigns and attacks, and was appointed governor of Qiansheng.
translations.idiomatic: In year 3 he reached Luoyang, campaigned with the army, and was named governor of Qiansheng.

sentences.zh: 六年，病，上書乞骸骨，徵詣京師。

translations.literal: In the sixth year he was ill, submitted a memorial begging for his bones, and was summoned to the capital.
translations.idiomatic: In year 6 illness drove him to ask retirement; he was recalled to the capital.

sentences.zh: 十三年，封為順陽侯。

translations.literal: In the thirteenth year he was enfeoffed as marquis of Shunyang.
translations.idiomatic: In year 13 he received Shunyang.

sentences.zh: 秋，復封嘉子廧為黃李侯。

translations.literal: In autumn he again enfeoffed Jia’s son Qiang as marquis of Huangli.
translations.idiomatic: That autumn his son Qiang was enfeoffed as marquis of Huangli.

sentences.zh: 十五年，嘉卒。

translations.literal: In the fifteenth year Jia died.
translations.idiomatic: Jia died in year 15.

sentences.zh: 子參嗣，有罪，削為南鄉侯。

translations.literal: His son Can succeeded; having guilt, he was reduced to marquis of Nanxiang.
translations.idiomatic: His heir Can lost rank for a crime and became marquis of Nanxiang.

sentences.zh: 永平中，參為城門校尉。

translations.literal: In the Yongping period Can became colonel of the gates.
translations.idiomatic: Under Yongping he served as colonel of the gates.

sentences.zh: 參卒，子循嗣。

translations.literal: When Can died, his son Xun succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Can was followed by Xun.

sentences.zh: 循卒，子章嗣。

translations.literal: When Xun died, his son Zhang succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Xun was followed by Zhang.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：齊武沈雄，義戈乘風。

translations.literal: The encomium says: Prince Martial of Qi was deep and mighty; with righteous halberds he rode the wind.
translations.idiomatic: The summation sings of Prince Martial of Qi: deep valor, righteous arms riding the gale.

sentences.zh: 倉卒匪圖，亡我天工。

translations.literal: In haste he did not plan; Heaven’s work perished for us.
translations.idiomatic: Rash, without a long design, Heaven’s favor slipped through his fingers.

sentences.zh: 城陽早協，趙孝晚同。

translations.literal: Chengyang early joined in harmony; Zhao Xiao late shared the same.
translations.idiomatic: Chengyang pledged early; Zhao Xiao came late to the same cause.

sentences.zh: 泗水三侯，或恩或功。

translations.literal: The three marquises of Sishui—some by grace, some by merit.
translations.idiomatic: The three lords of Sishui earned their titles by kindness or by deeds.
